title: Anger At History Books That Erased Your People
dek: When the curriculum sanitizes your ancestry, the body remembers the truth. Your fury is not a political statement; it is a biological imperative demanding to be felt in the fascia and the bone.
bodyMarkdown:
You pick up the history book, its spine stiff and unfamiliar under your fingers, the pages whispering promises of stories long told. But as you read, the silence begins. The names you know—your ancestors, their lives, their pain—are nowhere to be found. Instead, there’s a glossing over, a smooth erasure that feels like a slow burn behind your eyes. The masseter muscle, that thick band along your jawline, clenches without your permission. Your teeth grind just slightly, a private protest your body mounts before your mind fully registers the betrayal.

Your breath shallows, caught high in the diaphragm, refusing to expand as it should. You notice the subtle hitch beneath your ribs, where the diaphragm’s dome should dip, making room for air to flood in. Instead, it tightens, a visceral contraction that speaks louder than any words on the page. The levator scapulae at the back of your neck stiffens, pulling your head slightly forward, as if trying to shield you from the erasure. You sit there, the evening light fading through the window, the room cooling, but inside you, a fire ignites—slow, deep, and undeniable.

This isn’t simply disappointment. This is biology. Your body knows what your mind is still trying to comprehend: to be unseen, to have your story wiped clean or rewritten, is a wound that does not just touch your heart but digs into your muscles, your fascia, your breath. The jaw remembers what your eyes refuse to accept. This anger, this tightening, this shallow breath—it’s not a failure of character or an overreaction. It’s a profound, embodied response to erasure.

Teachers like Liz Lipski remind us that the body holds memory in every cell, and what you’re feeling now is a conversation your ancestors’ bodies started long ago—about survival, about being made invisible, about the refusal to be forgotten. So when the history books erase your people, your body reacts with the truth it carries, with muscle and breath, with anger that is as natural as it is necessary. This is not pathology. This is biology honoring what the page denies.

## When the Body Remembers What the Books Don’t Say

You sit in the quiet of a late spring evening, the air thick with the scent of damp earth and blooming lilac, and suddenly something stirs deep inside you. It’s not a thought you can pin down, not a story handed down in words or written in any family album. It’s a muscle—your psoas—that contracts with a sharp, almost electric memory. This is the body’s archive, the place where the silent histories of your ancestors live, even when the books are quiet.

Resmaa Menakem, whom I’ve studied closely, calls this somatic abolitionism. He teaches that trauma and resilience are not only passed down through stories or culture but are carried in the very fascia and muscle fibers of our bodies. The body does not lie. It remembers the upheaval of fleeing violence, the constriction of unspoken grief, the clenched jaw of survival. When the trapezius tightens along your neck and shoulders, it is not simply stress—it is the echo of a lineage’s burden, the subtle imprint of ancestors who had to hold themselves small, still, and silent to navigate a world that did not want their full presence.

You feel your masseter jaw muscle tighten as well, that same clenched readiness that protected your grandmother from harsh words she never voiced aloud. You might think these are just habits, but this is not pathology. This is biology. The body encodes these tensions like a ledger of survival strategies. And sometimes, in the dead of night when the world softens and your breathing deepens, the psoas—a muscle often called the “muscle of the soul”—contracts sharply. It’s the body’s way of saying: I remember the running, the hiding, the fierce will to protect what mattered most. 

This waking is not a curse but a call to witness. You learn to listen to the subtle throbbing in your trapezius, the tightening in your diaphragm, the whispered song of resilience held in your fascia. This is where healing begins—not in rewriting history but in honoring the body’s truth, in making space for the fullness of what your ancestors carried with them. The body knows. The jaw remembers. And in this remembering, you find grounding, connection, and the deep medicine of presence.

## When the Curriculum Cracks and the Anger Ignites

You remember the exact moment the curriculum cracked—the moment you saw through the carefully constructed lie that told you your fury was ugly, unwomanly, something to be silenced or shamed. It hits you like a sudden heat in the center of your chest, like a flare igniting just beneath the sternum, where the ribs frame a tight, pulsing core. That’s liver qi, stuck and rebellious, a current that wants to surge but is trapped behind a dam of old stories and silencing. The heat is not abstract; it knifes through the diaphragm, tightening the muscle fibers in a way that makes your breath shallow and quick, as if the body is trying to outrun a warning flare.

Your levator scapulae, those narrow bands of muscle running from your neck to your shoulder blades, knot themselves into angry little fists, pulling your shoulders up toward your ears in a silent scream. There’s a sharpness in the trapezius, too, that feels like a wire pulled taut against your spine, the fascia thickening and resisting. This is not just metaphor—this is the body’s memory, the jaw clenched so tightly the masseter muscles throb with tension, the teeth grinding until the temples pulse with a dull, relentless ache. You might catch the faint scent of your own skin, a mix of sweat and something metallic, like the coppery edge of anger freshly spilled.

This is not pathology, this is biology. The liver, in Traditional Chinese Medicine, is the general of the body’s energy, responsible for the smooth flow of qi and blood. When anger is stifled, liver qi stagnates, and heat rises, igniting the body from within. This heat can burn like a wildfire or simmer like a low-grade fever, depending on how long you’ve held it inside.

One of the most reliable allies I’ve found for moving this stagnant liver qi is the gentle bitterness of [Herb Pharm Mind Soother (formerly Anxiety Soother)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B008CPAKO6?tag=spankyspinola-20). The roots are earthy and grounding, a medicine that cools liver heat and encourages the flow without forcing it. Brewing a cup in the early evening—when the sky is bruising purple and the air smells faintly of rain—you’ll feel the warmth spread through your abdomen, loosening the tightness in your diaphragm and softening the grip in your neck. This isn’t a quick fix or a cleansing trend; it’s an invitation to listen to the body’s wisdom, to honor the fire that’s been dismissed for so long.

As your breath deepens and the muscles unclench, you start to recognize that anger isn’t your enemy. It’s a signal, a messenger from the body that the curriculum was a lie, and you deserve to be furious.

## When the Jaw Clenches on a Lie: The Body’s Reckoning with Suppressed Fury

It’s 3:17 a.m., and the air in your room smells faintly of chamomile steeped too long—sweet but bitter at the edges. Your masseter muscle, that thick slab running from your cheekbone to your jaw, clenches tight. You’re not asleep, though your body aches for it. This tension is a ledger, a record of every time you swallowed your fury because you were taught that anger was ugly, dangerous, or unlovable. Gabor Maté calls this “the body keeping the score,” and he’s right. The body knows when it’s being lied to. It knows when you’re pretending that everything is fine, when your history—the very moments that made you who you are—is being erased or minimized so you can be the “good, quiet student” who doesn’t disrupt the classroom or the family dinner.

That jaw tension isn’t just a random spasm; it’s a physical expression of a history of betrayal. Not the dramatic kind, necessarily, but the subtle, ongoing erasure of your truth. When you suppress that valid anger, the diaphragm—the muscle that should rise and fall with the natural rhythm of your breath—tightens like a spring wound too far. You might notice it as a shallow breath, as if you’re afraid to take up space in the world. The levator scapulae and trapezius muscles at the back of your neck knot themselves into painful reminders that you’ve been carrying the weight of silence for too long. This is not pathology; this is biology telling you, in the only language it knows, that it’s time to stop lying to yourself.

Suppressing anger is not just an emotional cost; it’s a somatic one. Your psoas muscles—deep core stabilizers—contract reflexively when you’re anxious or fearful, conditions frequently born out of long-term emotional suppression. Over time, these muscles harden into a fortress around your heart’s capacity to feel and express, locking away the fury in a cage that tightens with each “good, quiet” nod to authority. You can live like this for years, even decades, until the body refuses to hold the lie any longer. Illness creeps in, often in places you hadn’t connected to your story before: autoimmune disease, chronic fatigue, migraines, gut dysfunction. The body remembers what the mind forgets.

You’ve learned to be quiet, to shrink, to fold yourself small, but your body—the jaw, the psoas, the diaphragm—remembers every betrayal, every silenced scream. This is the medicine you need to hear: your fury is an archive, not a threat. It’s a map back to yourself, if only you can listen.

## Shaking Out the Jaw’s Quiet Fury

It’s just past midnight, the sky heavy with the damp chill of early spring rain pressing against your windowpane. You lie awake, jaw clenched so tight the masseter muscle screams in quiet rebellion. The body knows this fury—rooted deep in the tissues around your ears and creeping down your neck into the stubborn levator scapulae. This is not pathology, this is biology: your jaw remembers every insult, every dismissal, every time you swallowed your fury to keep the peace.

Tonight, you’re going to move that anger. First, settle your feet flat on the floor, grounding yourself into the cold hard wood. Feel your arches press down, the subtle engagement of the tibialis anterior and the slow unwinding tension in the calves. This is your anchor. Now bring your hands gently to your face, fingertips resting just below your cheekbones, where the masseter muscle bulges when you clench. Take a slow inhalation, filling your diaphragm fully—a deep breath that expands the belly and ribs, the familiar stretch and release of the diaphragm muscle.

As you exhale, open your mouth wide—not a polite smile, but a real, unguarded yawn. Let your jaw hang heavy, the temporomandibular joint loosening, the muscles around your ears stretching and softening. Imagine the fascia between the masseter and the skin melting like wax, easing the grip of tension. On your next breath in, hum low and steady, feeling the vibration travel through your throat, the vocal cords pulsing like a slow drumbeat. This sound isn’t pretty or controlled—it’s raw and honest, the voice your fury was never allowed to claim.

Now comes the shaking. With your feet still grounded, start to gently shake your head from side to side, letting the weight of your jaw pull against gravity. The neck muscles—sternocleidomastoid, trapezius—begin to loosen their iron clasp. Feel the subtle release in the fascia wrapping your cervical vertebrae, the quiet uncoiling of tension that wraps from the base of the skull down your spine. Let your shoulders drop away from your ears, the levator scapulae sighing in relief.

This is not a performance. It’s a reckoning with the stored fury, the body’s archive of all the times you were told to be quiet, to be small. The shaking doesn’t stop until you feel the jaw soften completely, the masseter muscle no longer a clenched fist but a relaxed hand resting gently against your face. When you finally still, notice the space in your throat where the hum lived, the calm beneath the storm. This is your body remembering freedom—one slow, shaking breath at a time.

## Living with the Fury of Erasure: An Ongoing Practice of Honoring Your Body’s Truth

You wake in the quiet, when the world still smells of night and damp earth, the dampness lingering in the grooves of your trapezius and the subtle tension threaded through your levator scapulae. The fury that has been lodged in your jaw—masseter clenched tight against years of ‘don’t be angry,’ ‘don’t take up space’—is not some pathological flaw. It’s a living archive, a muscle memory that remembers the erasure you endured, the silencing you survived. This is not pathology, this is biology. Your body is speaking a language that the textbooks, with their neat categories and clinical detachment, refuse to translate.

The integration of this anger is not about erasing it or stuffing it back into the dark corners of your diaphragm where it has settled like a shadow. The integration is a daily, sometimes hourly, commitment to recognizing the truth your body tells you, even when society whispers that fury is ugly, that your rage is inconvenient or excessive. You do not have to become consumed by this fire. You learn to live with it as a fierce, loyal companion—one that demands your attention and respect, not your repression.

Part of this ongoing practice is creating space for the nervous system to settle, even while the embers of your fury glow beneath the surface. Here, [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54ESK?tag=spankyspinola-20) becomes a vital ally. It’s not a sedative that dulls the edges of your aliveness; rather, it’s a tender, intelligent herb that eases the frayed threads of your nervous system. When you take it, the subtle release in your diaphragm is palpable, like a breath held too long finally escaping through the soft tissues. The levator scapulae loosens its grip, the masseter unclenches—this is not about numbing your anger, but allowing your body to hold it without collapsing under its weight.

Your teachers—like Jill Blakeway or Stephen Buhner—remind you that honoring the body’s truth means listening to these sensations without judgment. The textbook’s lie says anger is dangerous, uncontrollable, something to be fixed or hidden. Your body knows better. Your jaw remembers. The tension in your psoas, the tight coil of your diaphragm, the sharp alertness in your trapezius—they tell a story that must be honored, not erased.

So you practice. You breathe deep. You sip [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) as twilight settles in, feeling the night air cool your skin, carrying the scent of damp pine and earth. You remind yourself: your fury is a trace of your survival, a fierce pulse of your aliveness. You do not have to be consumed by it, but you must never silence it again.

Q: Why does my body tighten up when I think about erased histories?  
A: Your body tightens because the muscles remember what your mind hasn’t had a chance to fully register. That clench in your masseter—the jaw muscle that clamps down when you’re holding back words you weren’t allowed to say—is your body’s archive of silenced fury. The trapezius and levator scapulae in your neck and shoulders coil up like a spring at 4 a.m., when the world is quiet but your nervous system is loud with old injustice. This isn’t pathology; this is biology. Your body is carrying the weight of stories never told, and it’s showing up as tension, shallow breathing, an ache behind your eyes. Recognizing this is the first step toward unburdening yourself.  

Q: How do I work with this anger when it feels overwhelming and all-consuming?  
A: You don’t have to armor up or shove it down. Instead, start by noticing where in your body it lives—maybe a tight diaphragm that catches your breath, or a psoas muscle that clenches when you remember a history rewritten to exclude you. The body knows before the mind can catch up. Teachers like Peter Levine remind us that trauma and rage are stored in the fascia and muscle; the jaw remembers what the history books deny. You can begin by gently moving those areas—slow neck rolls, deep belly breaths that massage the diaphragm, or a weighted blanket that soothes the trapezius. Let your hands be medicine carriers too: holding a cup of chamomile tea, feeling the warm earthiness of turmeric on your tongue, can ground you. This isn’t about “fixing” your anger; it’s about witnessing it as a living force within your body.  

Q: Are there herbs that can help when the anger feels stuck or too intense?  
A: Yes—herbs are allies, not magic wands. The bitter and cooling qualities of skullcap and lemon balm can ease the nervous system’s overdrive, helping the diaphragm to relax and the breath to deepen. Hawthorn berry, with its slow, steady heart-opening action, supports the physical and emotional resilience you need to carry this work. And if the jaw is clenched tight, a tincture of wild cherry bark can soften that masseter muscle’s grip. Remember, though, that herbs are part of a conversation with your body, not a one-way fix. They meet you where you are, honoring the reality of your anger and the slow unraveling of its hold.  

Q: How do I stop feeling guilty for being angry about things that happened before I was born?  
A: Guilt is a tight knot in your solar plexus, the place where personal and collective histories meet. Your anger is not misplaced; it’s a direct response to erasure and injustice that runs through your family and your body’s lineage. The diaphragm and psoas muscles hold this intergenerational memory, and your body is simply reacting to what it knows. When you release the judgment around your anger, you create space for healing. This is not about blame; it’s about acknowledgment. As herbalist Susun Weed says, “The body is a sacred text.” Your fury is a chapter that demands to be read and honored.  

Q: Can somatic practices and herbs replace therapy or other forms of healing?  
A: They complement but do not replace. Somatic work and herbs address the biology of your anger—the muscle tension, the nervous system holding patterns, the subtle shifts in fascia and breath. Therapy or community support can help unpack the stories and offer new narratives. Think of herbs and bodywork as the groundwork, the tending of the soil where new growth can begin. Together, they form a resilient scaffold for your healing, one that respects both your body’s memory and your mind’s capacity to understand.
